Title: Cytokinin Production by the Rice Blast Fungus Is a Pivotal Requirement for Full Virulence

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: native CKS1 promoter (genomic complementation); endogenous promoter, constitutive/native context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in wild type GY11 and complemented strain in vitro; not detected in cks1 deletion mutants by qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in wild type GY11 and complemented strain during in vitro culture; absent in deletion mutant
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: cytokinin biosynthesis via the tRNA-degradation/tRNA-modification route
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream; first step of the tRNA-mediated CK biosynthesis pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for cytokinin production
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: tRNA-bound adenine residues; dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMAPP); ATP-binding motif conserved
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: isopentenylated tRNA leading to cytokinin nucleotides/ribosides after tRNA turnover
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an isopentenyl group from DMAPP to adenine in specific tRNAs, generating isopentenylated tRNA precursors that give rise to cytokinins upon tRNA degradation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: cytokinins (major detected forms: cisZR, cisZNT, iPR, iPNT)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: In this pathosystem, fungal-derived cytokinins dampen host defense, reduce oxidative burst, alter host cytokinin signaling, and help maintain sugar and key amino-acid availability during infection
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: fungal mycelia and culture supernatant; functionally at/around rice infection sites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for fungal cytokinin biosynthesis and secretion; deletion abolishes detectable CK production and reduces virulence, penetration, oxidative-stress tolerance, and host nutrient manipulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step; the only identified IPT-domain protein in M. oryzae and deletion abolishes detectable cytokinin production
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: minimal medium without yeast extract for CK measurement; oxidative stress with 1 mM H2O2; exogenous kinetin or cis-zeatin restores mutant defects; rice infection context; high nitrogen fertilization affects interpretation of virulence/nutrient effects
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion causes reduced virulence and slight hypersensitivity to oxidative stress, but no obvious growth or appressorium defects under standard in vitro conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene knockout by homologous recombination; genomic complementation under native promoter; qRT-PCR confirmation; LC(ESI+)-MS/MS cytokinin profiling; virulence assays on rice; exogenous cytokinin rescue; oxidative-stress assays; metabolite profiling of infected leaves
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MGG_04857/CKS1 encodes the only identifiable IPT-domain protein in Magnaporthe oryzae and is predicted to be a bona fide tRNA-IPT. 2. cks1 knockout mutants produced no detectable cytokinins (cisZNT, iPNT, cisZR, iPR) in mycelia or supernatant, while complementation restored production. 3. Loss of CKS1 reduced fungal penetration and virulence on rice, increased host oxidative burst and defense-gene induction, and altered sugar and amino-acid dynamics at infection sites. 4. Exogenous kinetin or cis-zeatin restored virulence, strongly linking the phenotype to loss of cytokinin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper infers but does not directly biochemically prove enzyme activity; assignment is based on conserved motifs/structure, unique IPT-domain occurrence, and knockout phenotype. Major fungal CKs detected were cis-zeatin forms. Source and applied species are the same fungus for pathway validation, but the metabolic consequences were examined in infected rice tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: targeted cytokinin metabolite profiling by LC(ESI+)-MS/MS; targeted sugar/amino-acid metabolite profiling; qRT-PCR of host marker genes; no accession number reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CKS1 is a potential antifungal target for disease control because disrupting fungal cytokinin biosynthesis lowers virulence without strongly impairing basal fungal growth; caution that host nutrient status can partially compensate for virulence loss
